trajectory_summary: The call opens with a conditionally open customer who has a specific product expectation baked in from the ad. Corey runs a competent verify sequence but never addresses the Tesla expectation that surfaces at 00:21 and resurfaces fatally at 03:11. When Greg's soft withdrawal arrives at 03:40, Corey exits immediately with no reframe attempt. The call ends as a lost lead that was recoverable — Greg's 'probably not interested' was a soft door, not a slammed one.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the correct classification is 'no appointment booked / lost lead.' The call ends with a mutual goodbye at 03:47 and no appointment, no callback, no tentative next step. The lead is lost.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Tesla expectation gap — reframe vs. confirm-and-exit
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Unfortunately, not. So those have been back ordered for the better half of this decade. We're probably not gonna see them for a minute just because nobody has them right now with them being so far back ordered.
primary_coaching_focus.why: This is the moment the call was lost, and it was recoverable. Corey had the Tesla relationship reframe available (schema: context.tesla_relationship_clarification) and didn't deploy it — confirming the product mismatch as bad news with no alternative value. The coaching sequence runs: (1) confirm whether Corey knows the Tesla referral relationship and Ion's actual catalog; (2) if knowledge gap, fill it first; (3) then drill the reframe move at 03:11 and the changed_mind rebuttal at 03:40 as the two-step recovery. The upstream fix is the Tesla intro at 00:04 — but the 03:26 moment is where the call was actually lost and where the rep needs the most concrete alternative.

key_moments.quote: Great. This is Corey with Ion. I, gotten a request here you're looking at solar for your home. Is that right?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a Tesla-sourced lead — Greg's first substantive turn confirms it ('the ad I responded to said that Tesla was looking for people'). Corey uses the standard intro instead of the Tesla intro template. The Tesla intro ('we're the certified solar installer for Tesla in {state}') would have established the referral relationship immediately and pre-empted Greg's product expectation from forming unchecked. This is the upstream failure that makes the 00:21 and 03:11 moments structurally inevitable.

key_moments.quote: The ad I responded to said that Tesla was looking for people that, no. The ad kinda inferred that there was no cost involved.
key_moments.stewart_read: Greg's self-correction mid-sentence ('Tesla was looking for people that, no') is the first signal of the Tesla roof-tile expectation. Corey hears only the 'no cost' surface concern and responds to that alone. The Tesla product signal is present and unaddressed. Strategic read: Corey may have judged the Tesla signal as too vague to address directly at this stage. Reactive read: Corey missed the signal entirely and defaulted to the cost clarification. The reactive read is more supported — there is no later move that picks up the Tesla thread before 03:11.

key_moments.quote: I'll confirm because Thursday is Tuesday. Yes. There isn't any cost involved for, like, the installation. So there isn't any upfront cost, but that doesn't mean the equipment itself is free.
key_moments.stewart_read: The first clause ('there isn't any cost involved for the installation') is schema-compliant legitimacy establishment — it addresses Greg's 'no cost' concern directly. The second clause ('but that doesn't mean the equipment itself is free') is the narrow coaching gap: it's an unsolicited disclosure that opens a cost-anxiety door Corey then has to manage. This is not scope creep by the schema definition (which covers closer-tier technical content), but it is minor over-disclosure — volunteering a potential objection before the customer raised it.

key_moments.quote: Well, you know, it varies greatly. It was almost, like, it was, like, $3.84 last month.
key_moments.stewart_read: The transcript reads '$3.84 last month' — this is almost certainly a Deepgram transcription artifact for $384, but per the schema's numeric_discipline directive, this must be flagged explicitly: confirm against the actual bill or CRM record before using this number in any coaching language. Assuming the figure is $384: Corey's response ('Oh, jeez. Yeah. I can definitely see why you're looking into this') is an acknowledgment but not a flip. The bill is captured and dropped — the schema's 'ask + acknowledge + flip' pattern is truncated at acknowledge. No emotional alignment-against-the-utility is built.
